Paid tier users will have limited monthly download caps with the ability to pay for more downloads."},{"url":"https://www.digitalmusicnews.com/2025/12/22/suno-warner-music-deal-changes/","title":"Suno Previews 2026 Changes Under Warner Music Deal","content":"One month after partnering with Warner Music Group (WMG), Suno is shedding light on the direction of its planned changes for 2026 – including a fresh emphasis on subscriber-exclusive monetization and an adjusted output-ownership policy.
But they did indicate that Suno would launch “more advanced and licensed models” and implement “monthly download caps,” among other things."},{"url":"https://jackrighteous.com/en-us/blogs/guides-using-suno-ai-music-creation/protect-your-suno-music-catalog-2026","title":"Protect Your Suno Catalog Before the 2026 Shift – Jack Righteous","content":"Suno’s new licensing direction with Warner Music Group has sparked a lot of debate.
Nothing here removes your rights to the music you created as a paid user."},{"url":"https://www.chartlex.com/blog/marketing/ai-music-generator-comparison-2026","title":"AI Music Generator Comparison 2026: Suno vs Udio + 3 More | Chartlex","content":"Last verified: 2026-07-11 · Refresh cadence: monthly (active AI music story).
Per the June 30, 2026 amended scheduling order in the Massachusetts case (Dkt.
The original legal trigger: in June 2024, Sony Music, Universal Music Group, and Warner Records, coordinated by the RIAA, sued Suno (District of Massachusetts) and Udio (Southern District of New York) for training on copyrighted recordings without permission (official RIAA announcement).
Udio settled with Universal Music Group in October 2025 (official announcement), then signed deals with Warner (November 2025), Merlin (January 2026), and Kobalt (April 2026).
ElevenLabs Music launched in August 2025 built on licensing partnerships, including opt-in deals with Merlin and Kobalt, plus a Music Marketplace where rights holders can monetize.
The company hit a $2.45B valuation in its November 2025 round, with a reported $300M annualized recurring revenue and roughly 2 million paid subscribers as of February 2026 (TechCrunch).
Under the Warner deal, Suno has said that when its new licensed models launch in 2026, free-tier songs will no longer be downloadable and paid tiers will get monthly download caps (WMG announcement).
As part of the UMG partnership changes implemented October 29, 2025, downloading of audio, video, and stems has been disabled, per Udio's official help center.
Udio has the stronger settlement record (UMG, Warner, Merlin, Kobalt), but all downloads including stems have been disabled since October 29, 2025 while its licensed platform is built, and it still faces the Sony case."}]}

Suno AI just partnered with Warner Music Group — and for Suno users, that means new rules are coming: free vs paid downloads, opt-in artists, and a roadmap pointing toward 2026. In this video I break it down clearly, answer the biggest questions people are asking, and give you a simple action plan so you’re not caught off guard.

You’ll learn:

✅ What Suno x Warner actually announced (no fluff)
✅ What changes for Free vs Paid users (especially downloads)
✅ What opt-in means (names, images, voice/likeness, compositions)
✅ Older songs + monetization: what we know vs what’s unclear

In late 2025, the music industry reached its "Napster moment" for AI. If you're planning to drop a Suno track on Spotify or Apple Music in 2026, the "Is it legal?" question has a very specific, tiered answer. //The 2026 reality: Ownership language has shifted from 'User Owned' to 'Granted Commercial Rights' for paid subscribers. Anything generated on a free account is for "personal, non-commercial use" only. This allows you to distribute the tracks to Spotify and Apple Music and keep 100% of the royalties. Even as a paid subscriber, Suno technically remains the "author" of the audio, while you are granted a perpetual license to exploit it commercially. While you can *monetize* a Suno track, you cannot *copyright* it in its raw form.
